figures.caption: Figure 1: CADENCE overview. Stage 1 maps each high-dimensional observation xi ti to a latent state zi ti via a score-based bijective PF-ODE, Gaussian-pinning the latent space. Stage 2 routes the realization’s static context ci through the SMoE gating network to produce a convex expert mixture wi, which conditions the Neural ODE. Forward integration yields individual future trajectories.

Table 2: MAE and SW2 at prediction horizon H=20. Note asymmetric data access: to provide strong empirical upper bounds, all baselines are given an informational advantage and trained on full longitudinal trajectories (italicized), with the strongest oracle baseline (OT-CFM) underlined. CADENCE is trained on sparse cross-sectional data, yet achieves competitive performance.

Figure 2: BM1 results comparing CADENCE against published baselines. (a) Per-expert predicted trajectories vs. ground truth. (b) Mean routing accuracy matrix: diagonal confirms effective subgroup prediction. (c) RMSE across the prediction horizon.

Figure 3: BM6 results comparing CADENCE against published baselines. (a) Per-expert predicted trajectories vs. ground truth. (b) Mean routing accuracy matrix: diagonal confirms effective subgroup prediction. (c) RMSE across the prediction horizon.

Figure 4: BM7 (LARRY haematopoiesis) results comparing CADENCE against published baselines. (a) Fate prediction accuracy: CADENCE discovers Neutrophil vs. Monocyte fates unsupervised, outperforming all baselines including scDiffEq (67.8% vs. 58.5%). (b) Per-day Sinkhorn divergence: CADENCE achieves the lowest population-level distributional distance at all evaluation time points. (d) Fate probability Pearson correlation against clonal ground truth. (d) Comparison of observed and CADENCE predicted clonal evolution trajectories.
